--

Small project with perfect modules that died before release vs fast MVP that made money. What to choose and when.
A story about two developers, one deadline, and the business that wasn’t happy with either of them.

Every developer has been there. A new project lands on your desk and before you write a single line of code, the question hits you: what architecture do I use?
Some answer on autopilot — they go with what they know, what worked last time, the path of least resistance. Others open twelve tabs, read four articles, and spend three days choosing between approaches they’ve never actually shipped. Both think they’re doing the right thing.
They’re both wrong. Here’s why.

Two Developers. Same Task. Same Deadline.
Imagine two developers — let’s call them Dev A and Dev B — both receive the same project. Medium-sized mobile app, meant to test user response in a new niche. They get the same inputs: design files, ad unit IDs, Firebase config. No detailed task breakdown (welcome to small and medium business), just a goal and a deadline.
Dev A does it right. He analyzes the task, estimates time, documents risks he’ll “definitely never encounter,” and picks an architecture he’s been wanting to try. Clean, scalable, impressive on paper.
Dev B opens Android Studio and starts writing. He’s done this before. He picks the simplest path that he knows works and moves.
Four weeks later, Dev A ships. Three weeks later, Dev B ships.
Both are proud. Both think they nailed it.
And yet — the business isn’t fully happy with either of them.

Why?
Because neither of them asked the right question before starting.
Not ”which architecture should I use?”
But ”what does this project actually need to become?”
There’s a fundamental difference between a project built to last and a project built to test. And the mistake most developers make — junior and senior alike — is treating every project the same way.
Dev A over-engineered a test. He built a cathedral when the business needed a tent to see if anyone would show up.
Dev B under-thought a product. He moved fast but left no foundation for what comes next if the test succeeds.

The Third Developer
Now imagine a third developer walks into either of these companies and gets the same task.
He doesn’t immediately open Stack Overflow to find the most upvoted architecture. He doesn’t start coding from muscle memory either.
He does something simple that most developers skip: he talks to the business first.
Not a long meeting. Not a requirements document. Just a few direct questions:
- Is this a test or a long-term product?
- If it works, how fast do you need to scale?
- What’s the real deadline — not the optimistic one?
- Are there parts of the design that might change after first user feedback?
Ten minutes of this conversation tells him everything he needs to know about architecture. Not the other way around.

The Actual Rule
Here’s the framework that actually works in the real world:
For test projects — apps meant to validate a niche, check user activity, or get quick market feedback — choose simplicity. Single module. Proven stack you know cold. This is actually the perfect time to try a new library or component you’ve been curious about, as long as it doesn’t blow the timeline. The goal is to ship and learn, not to impress other developers with your folder structure.
For long-term products — apps the business plans to grow, add features to, hand off to other developers — invest in architecture upfront. Modular structure, clean separation of layers, decomposed tasks. Not because it’s beautiful, but because it saves ten times the time later.
The trap is applying long-term thinking to short-term projects, and short-term thinking to long-term products.

What About Business?
Here’s something most developers don’t realize: business wants you to grow.
If you come to a manager and say ”I want to try this new approach on this project, it’ll take me X extra days” — most of the time, they’ll say yes. Especially if you’re honest about the tradeoff.
What they don’t want is to find out three weeks in that you spent the first week debating architecture in your head, or that the project needs to be rebuilt before it even launches.
Communicate the key points. Agree on the timeline. Then execute.
Don’t be afraid to experiment — just don’t miss the deadline while doing it.

The One-Line Summary
Architecture is a tool. Use the right one for the job — not the most impressive one, not the most familiar one. The right one.
A perfect architecture that ships late has already lost to a simple one that shipped on time.

This is the first article in a series about pragmatic development for real business. Not theory — patterns from actual projects in small and medium companies.
